sentences.text: December 22, 1941.
No.
The first evening, what happened, somebody came, and he had a [INAUDIBLE] camp police and informed of 500 there would be no food for us.
Because he had not assigned any food for the newcomers.
And for the first three days, we did not get any food except a couple of lumps of sugar.
Also, we were informed that we could not make any fires.
There was no water for washing or drinking.
You could eat snow, or in the morning and the evening, there would be coffee.
Everyone was in shock.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was cold.
We had only one blanket each.
And we slept as close together as possible to stay warm.
And then we were informed that nobody could leave the barracks, only if you had to go to the bathroom. 10 Jews had to be collected.
We had to call out to the guards.
He would march you to the toilet.
And he would march you back.
Anyone leaving the barracks without the guard would be shot outside.
On the next morning, we were assigned work to carry lumber from the saw mill into the camp.
And I did that for about a week with my brothers.
Then, on December 30, my brother got assigned as group leader for the burial detail.
Because there was other epidemic going on of diarrhea.
Because the water was partially taken from the River Duina and was polluted, and it was not boiled enough to kill the bacteria in it.
So a lot of people died from the diarrhea and dehydration.
And they needed burial detail, and he was assigned to it.
Yes, we stayed together.
But he had to dig graves in day time.
Which was very difficult, because the ground was frozen.
And he was made the group leader, I said, for the barrack number two.
There was a barrack number one, with a group leader was-- and Eric [NON-ENGLISH] from Hamburg.
And then we would receive 300 grams of bread, which is close to 11 ounces.
But it was everything except bread.
What was in it, I don't know.
It was like clay, and you had to watch out that you wouldn't bite on a stone or something.
Dirt was in it.
And you did not always have your 11 ounces, either.
Once.
In the evening, bread.
At noon time, you were given soup, which-- you were lucky if you had a potato or something in it.
Let's say it was hot water in the morning and the evening was so-called coffee.
Which was from grain, and it was pee warm.
And you could get sick from it, because it was not boiled from the water.
There was no roll call.
There was nothing.
You were assigned-- when you had a group leader, they took you.
Nobody cared if you stayed behind or you worked, at that time.
And then the first big surprise we got was on January 2, 1942.
We heard that two Jews had run away from the camp.
They're name was [NON-ENGLISH] and Herskovits, and they were from the transport of Hanover.
And that morning, an SS sturmbannfuhrer, or major, came in the camp and said that two Jews from barrack two had escaped and told the barrack leader, Wietan, to select 10 Jews to be shot.
And Wietan told him, sorry, you have only one.
That's me.
The rest, you have to pick yourself.
And we thought he was a hero.
But later on, it turned out that he was not a hero.
Anyway, we were told to line up, the whole camp, in a form of a horseshoe.
1,000 were in that camp by that time.
Yeah.
Supposed to be, except for the dead ones, which already had died.
And we had to stay there for a couple of hours, and it was bitter cold.
Maybe 10, 15 below zero.
And a lot of frozen toes.
My brother had toes frozen up also.
And eventually, a couple of truckloads of SS came on.
They put up some machine guns in back of us, and other rifle men.
Then another truck came on, where the two Jews were on it and some other SS.
The two were put up on the open end of the horseshoe.
And four SS men, SS NCO-- they were Latvians-- were standing in back of them.
And they got executed.
It was one of the most ugly executions I've ever seen.
One had half the skull blown away.
The other one had shot through the neck, and the lower jaw was blown off.
And the blood was shooting out because the heart was still pumping.
Yes.
And then I got told to help to dig the drave for those two.
So I was assigned to the burial detail also.
Yeah.
And then my youngest brother got also diarrhea.
And we know that most of-- the doctor told us that most of them had died of dehydration.
So we melted some snow, even though it was dangerous.
Because if they catch you, they will shoot you and force water into him.
So he would get again dehydration.
And we got him through.
But then a few days later, a number of toes turned black.
He had gangrene from the frozen toes.
So we had brought some clothes in from the ghetto along.
And though we bribed a medicine student for a couple of shirts, which we could trade in then for food, to cut off the frozen toes.
Was the medical student in your barracks?
In our barracks, which he did.
And then, in the evening, we got another surprise.
Latvian SS came in the barracks, full with knapsacks of bread and butter.
And they wanted to trade the bread and butter for watches or for wedding bands.
They said, you may as well trade it in now because we will get it later on anyway, when you die of starvation.
But in the meantime, you will have a full belly.
And a lot of Jews traded their watches in for bread.
No, we did not, at that time.
We were hungry just like the rest of them.
And then-- my youngest brother couldn't walk, because he had the toes and everything.
So we hid him under the mattresses we were sleeping on, and nobody would see him.
Because there was no roll call or nothing going on, it worked.
Everyone was down in the dumps.
The way it looks is it's the end of the line.
And let's fight back as much as we can.
Trading food, whatever we have, our property, with the Latvians.
And even if we have to get rid of our watches or whatever we have eventually.
Yes, to survive.
We made some friends over there.
Some survived, some died.
There were some fellows I knew, we were together with before we got deported.
And then we got the first surprise when the commandant, it was an SS [NON-ENGLISH].
And when he saw a couple of fellows with a hunchback.
And he started raising hell.
What are they doing here?
This is a labor camp, not a sanatorium.
The next day, he had them shipped back to the ghetto.
So we thought, uh oh, that's-- we see daylight.
Maybe things will be changing.
And there was a physician-- I forgot his name-- in the camp.
He said, I'm going to talk to the chief of the kitchen.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
That he has to boil the water more, to get rid of the diarrhea.
Because this is all caused by polluted water only, and get these utensils clean.
But instead of talking to him, he got a beating.
And told to stay out of the kitchen.
Yes.
Also, a few weeks later, we had to line up, and there was Lange, the chief of the security police for Latvia standing and his [INAUDIBLE] Maiwald.
And there were two chairs.
There was a suitcase on it.
Maiwald said, in order that we can win the war, we need your watches and your wedding bands.
We have to throw that in a suitcase.
Anyone who will refuse to do so will be shot.
So we had to march by-- we wound our watches.
We broke the springs, pulled the crowns out.
A lot of people throw their wedding bands in the snow instead of giving it to the Germans and had to march by.
And some Latvian SS was checking here and there if they had followed the advice of the Germans.
Were there relationships between different types of Jews within this camp?
Between Jews from different geographic places?
Yes.
There were relationships.
But everyone was too hungry and too tired to do much.
And then we found out you wouldn't get 11 ounces of bread also, which we are supposed to have.
They shortchanged us.
Also, we found out that the ladles we got the soup with, some were bigger, and some were smaller.
And some raised hell.
And when the argument was going on, the camp commander [NON-ENGLISH] came on and said, what do you mean they're different?
From now on, everyone will get a quart of soup.
But that quart of soup was 90% water.
When the inmates became violently sick, was there medical attention?
Was there a--
There was supposed to be.
But there was no medication.
There was nothing.
And when they had diarrhea, some even blood in it, that was the end of the line.
There was no water.
You could not wash up.
There was nothing to drink, either, except for the coffee, which could give you diarrhea.
Yes.
Nothing.
There was no connection with the ghetto whatsoever.
There was no punishment whatsoever, except we had a camp police-- the leader of the camp police was [NON-ENGLISH] Kauffman.
He worked for [NON-ENGLISH].
He was mediocre.
And most of the camp police were decent, but there was one fellow from-- I think from Stuttgart, Stuttgart.
His name was Frank.
He was a cattle leader in Germany, and he treated everyone like cattle, and he was beating up right and left.
And otherwise, camp police did very little.
Some where from decent people.
Some were garbage.
And Einstein, who later on Kauffman made the decision.
Who was who and what was what?
And Kauffman would put a lot of friends in that group.
No.
We had group leaders.
But they had to do the work, just like anyone else.
There were no kapos at that time.
Yes and no.
You had to go to work.
And the rest, forget about it, since there was no water to wash.
Some people even didn't shave anymore.
And everyone was trading in what was even possible to get food.
Then we could not dig graves anymore.
It was frozen too much.
Then one day-- it must be in the second part of January-- an SS NCO named [NON-ENGLISH] showed up.
And he said, you're going to dig 36 holes below the frost lines, and we'll do some blasting.
We're making a mass grave over here.
And there were maybe 200 bodies or more lying outside in the snow already.
When we walked by, the crows were flying off the bodies, which were picking on the bodies on the faces, picking out the eyes and everything.
We made the 36 holes, and then he came on and we had to load 10 pounds of [INAUDIBLE] as an explosive in six holes.
Then he gave us six fuses.
We had to put the fuses in and put dirt in-- first dry dirt and then wet dirt, and let it sit for a while.
And then he gave us a lighter to light the fuses.
And he said, you better watch out which fuse you light first, because they're different lengths.
If you light the short one, you won't light the long ones anymore.
You will be blowing it up by yourself.
You even won't know about it.
So we blasted 36 holes on that day.
Then we had to dig it up and then make 36 holes again.
Dig it, get the dirt out.
And then another one.
So we blasted three times to make a mass grave big enough to hold 1,500 Jews.
That means everyone who was in the camp afterwards.
Did you know what those holes were for?
Yeah.
We knew it was a mass grave.
Yeah.
You were under no illusions.
Then we were supposed to take the shoes and other good clothing off the bodies and turn them over to the clothing depot.
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE] By April, there were 850 bodies outside.
Beginning of April.
Only digging on that mass grave.
And carrying the bodies out every day.
And we were supposed to get all the good clothing and everything in the depot for clothing depot.
But we did not.
I mean, we were not proud of it, but that was the only way to stay alive.
You would take the shoes off, or the jackets, and throw it over to the Latvian farmers when they came by.
And they would, in turn, throw some bread or something else.
So we had something to eat.
That's how we survived.
But if anyone got caught, he got shot.
Or they hanged him.
Now the shooting was the easiest way, as far as I'm concerned.
Hanging was the worst part.
Because they were always strangled in the gallows.
Getting illegal food.
That was a crime.
Nearly everyone who got hanged, I had to take from the gallows afterwards.
And one day, when I took them from the gallows, I saw the eyes were popped out, and all the blood vessels were broken in the eyes.
So I asked the doctor, how is that possible?
And the doctor told me, well, they have been strangled in the gallows.
They don't drop enough.
Therefore, the fellow Jews who were-- a Jew was a hangman.
He was from Vienna.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And I would say he had a pleasure doing the hanging.
And then the Jewish police had to pull the chair from under the feet of the person who got hanged.
Every one.
Every execution I witnessed, shooting or hanging.
The first mass execution I ever witnessed was middle of May 1945.
There were-- 15 Jews were brought in a camp. 15 or 16, I'm not sure of that.
And one of them got pulled up on the gallows.
And that was a Jew who had two stars on his clothing.
One in front and one in back.
We found out later on that he was a Latvian Jew.
And then 14 had to line up in one row.
And then the SS walked behind them and shot them.
Now comes the worst part.
One of the Jews, 14 or 15 year old boy named Frank-- he was from the [INAUDIBLE] of Dusseldorf-- let himself drop to the ground when the order came, fire.
And a fellow Jew named [INAUDIBLE] Wertheim-- we thought he was a hero when he opposed Lange to select Jews for an execution-- pointed out to the SS that this one had not been shot.
The SS put him on his feet, and they shot him.
But the majority of those who got executed were still alive, moving arms and legs.
So the SS walked between them and shot everyone who was moving.
Well, the last execution was-- for illegal food-- was in June 1942.
And there were also two from Hanover.
[INAUDIBLE] because I had to bury them.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the meantime, also, came the order any Jew who doesn't-- is unable to work-- will get only half the ration of bread.
And they enforced that, even though there was no roll call who would go to work or not.
Then came the order that they had to check the barracks, that no Jew was hiding in the barracks.
The SS gave the orders to the barrack leaders, things like that.
And I'm sorry to say, most of the barrack leaders followed the orders.
And Wertheim was one of them.
He sent the people who cleaned up the barracks into the bunks to check that nobody was hiding in the bunks and everything.
So they found my youngest brother there.
With the toes.
With the toes.
But they were partially healed up.
And so the decision was made he can work in the barrack.
And then came the order on April 24, April 25, 1942-- that we had to dig up the graves for the ones we had buried before and put those in a mass grave.
Before we had put the bodies in the mass grave, which were lying outside.
And my brother [INAUDIBLE] had to count how many bodies we put in.
And that was in the beginning of April.
And we put 850 bodies in that grave.
We put a couple of layers bodies in.
Then we had to throw quicklime on and then put more layers of bodies in.
We put 10 layers in.
And we had to dig up the grave.
And it was-- the snow was melting, and water was running into the holes as we were digging it.
And we all had open wounds on our hands from the frost bite and everything.
And my brother got a tetanus infection, and he died of it on April 27.
Kurt.
No.
The majority even didn't know how to say Kaddish.
And I said Kaddish a few times for those who were executed.
Then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In May-- must be around the 20s, I fainted due to malnutrition.
And the next day, they put me on a truck.
Because each time a group of sick ones were in a camp, they were taken out of the camp.
Now this is the worst part.
Because the first transport, they were taken out by bus.
We feel they come in a ghetto.
But they did not come in a ghetto.
They were laid out in front of the bus, shot.
The second transport, there was-- one fellow was taken by-- the father was taken by his son or somebody else towards the truck.
And an SS NCO named [NON-ENGLISH], who was the commandant for Jungfernhof, walked up to him and said, he is too sick, and put a bullet in his head, in my presence.
And then he gave the orders that all jackets and shoes to be removed from those who are going on the trucks.
And the temperature must have been between 20 or 30 below 0.
So we were under no illusions about what's happening.
And when the third transport had to go out, people were fighting it.
They didn't want to go.
But the camp administration put them on the trucks anyway.
And not one of those between January, February and March and April came into the ghetto.
They all were shot.
Because later on, the clothing came back in the depot.
And I came into the ghetto.
That was the first transport to come in the ghetto.
Yeah.
We were only 12 on that truck.
And then we were brought in the ghetto.
Usually, there are 50 or 60 taken out who are sick.
The first person I ran into was my sister.
And she got a shock when she saw me, because I had lost 40 pounds of weight.
And I told her that the oldest brother had died.
And then we were put in quarantine.
We were deloused and put in quarantine.
And they said that I have malnutrition and my blood pressure is too low, is under 100.
Then I could rejoin my family the next day.
And I got extra food rations.
And I stayed in the ghetto for two weeks.
And here, I found out that a lot of relatives had been taken out on the 26th of March, 1942.
And that over 90% of the people over 50 years, and over 90% of all the children, had been removed from the ghetto.
And they were supposed to go to [NON-ENGLISH] in a village on the River Duina.
But this was a joke.
They all got killed.
And when I was later on in Jungfernhof, come back to-- the same action happened at the same day.
And in charge of the whole thing was Heinz Jost, commandant of the security police for [NON-ENGLISH], what the German called [NON-ENGLISH], Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia, and White Russia.
The ghetto-- hardly any people.
I saw some ghetto police, another piece of paper on the street and a big wire-- barbed-wire fence.
That's all I saw.
They all were in shock when they saw me.
Same way.
And they were even more in shock when I told them what happened to my oldest brother.
And then they asked about other friends and relatives.
And I told them, I tell you, don't you know what happened to them?
Nobody in the ghetto knew what was going on in Salaspils.
11 miles.
